questions.question: 1. The execution of a loop typically starts with:

explanations.a: Correct. Initialization statements set up the loop variables before the loop starts.
explanations.b: Incorrect. The loop body is executed after initialization and condition checking.
explanations.c: Incorrect. The test condition is checked after initialization.
explanations.d: Incorrect. Update statements are executed after the loop body in each iteration.

questions.question: 2. The break statement is used to exit from:

explanations.a: Incorrect. The break statement does not exit an if statement directly; it is used in loops or switch statements.
explanations.b: Correct. The break statement is used to exit from a loop such as a for loop.
explanations.c: Incorrect. The break statement does not terminate the entire program.
explanations.d: Incorrect. The break statement does not exit the main() function.

questions.question: 3. A do-while loop is useful when we want the statements within the loop to be executed:

explanations.a: Incorrect. The loop may execute more than once depending on the condition.
explanations.b: Correct. A do-while loop always executes its body at least once before checking the condition.
explanations.c: Incorrect. It may execute only once if the condition is false after the first iteration.
explanations.d: Incorrect. Option b is the correct description.

questions.question: 4. In what sequence are initialization, testing, and execution of the body done in a do-while loop?

explanations.a: Correct. In a do-while loop, initialization happens first, then the body is executed, and then the condition is tested.
explanations.b: Incorrect. Initialization must occur before the body is executed.
explanations.c: Incorrect. Testing occurs after the body in a do-while loop.
explanations.d: Incorrect. Option a is the correct sequence.

questions.question: 5. Which of the following is not an infinite loop?

explanations.a: Incorrect. This code is an infinite loop because the condition is always true and there is no break.
explanations.b: Incorrect. This code is an infinite loop for the same reason as option a.
explanations.c: Correct. This code is not an infinite loop because the loop variable is updated so that the condition eventually becomes false.
explanations.d: Incorrect. This code is an infinite loop.

questions.question: 6. Which of the following statements is used to take control to the beginning of the loop?

explanations.a: Incorrect. 'Exit' is not a standard C statement for loops.
explanations.b: Incorrect. 'Break' exits the loop, not restart it.
explanations.c: Correct. 'Continue' skips the rest of the loop body and starts the next iteration.
explanations.d: Incorrect. Option c is the correct answer.

questions.question: 7. How many '*' will this code print?

explanations.a: Incorrect. The loop runs twice, so two '*' are printed.
explanations.b: Incorrect. The loop runs twice, not once.
explanations.c: Correct. The loop runs for i=1 and i=2, printing '*' twice.
explanations.d: Incorrect. The loop does not run three times.

questions.question: 8. Multiple initialization is not possible in a for loop.

explanations.a: Incorrect. Multiple initialization is possible in a for loop using commas.
explanations.b: Correct. You can initialize multiple variables in a for loop, separated by commas.

questions.question: 9. A for loop can be converted into an equivalent while loop.

explanations.a: Correct. Any for loop can be rewritten as a while loop with equivalent logic.
explanations.b: Incorrect. For loops and while loops are interchangeable in logic.

explanations.a: Incorrect. Loops can be nested inside each other in C and most programming languages.
